page: Google Cloud Platform c4n-standard-192 is an x86_64 architecture server instance featuring 192 dedicated virtual CPUs and 720.0 GB of system memory. This configuration provides a resource density of 3.75 GB of RAM per vCPU. The instance does not include local storage or integrated GPU hardware, meaning all storage requirements must be met via network-attached volumes. Additionally, it does not include any complimentary public IPv4 addresses.

Operating within the c4n family, this instance offers high resource density for large-scale deployments. The lack of bundled local NVMe storage and integrated GPUs represents a clear architectural tradeoff, positioning this server for workloads that rely entirely on network-attached storage and CPU-bound processing. From a qualitative cost-efficiency perspective, the high core count and dedicated CPU allocation make it suitable for consolidating multiple smaller workloads or running large, parallelized applications without the overhead of GPU hardware.

The c4n-standard-192 is designed for highly parallelized CPU-bound tasks. Appropriate workloads include large-scale web serving, continuous integration and continuous delivery (CI/CD) pipelines, distributed data processing, and general-purpose enterprise applications that require significant multi-core processing capabilities without specialized hardware acceleration.
description: Google Cloud Platform c4n-standard-192 is an x86_64 architecture server configured with 192 dedicated vCPUs and 720.0 GB of RAM, yielding a balanced memory-to-core ratio of 3.75 GB per vCPU. This instance does not include local storage or GPU accelerators, requiring reliance on network-attached storage for data persistence. It also provides zero complimentary public IPv4 addresses. Designed for high-density CPU workloads, the server offers qualitative cost efficiency by providing a massive pool of dedicated compute resources without the added expense of specialized hardware. The c4n-standard-192 is suited for large-scale general-purpose applications, distributed data processing, enterprise web serving, and parallelized CI/CD pipelines that demand substantial multi-core processing power.
